title: Pressure-cooker chickpea curry
subtitle: Dried chickpeas from scratch in under an hour, finished with garam masala and coriander.
excerpt: Dried chickpeas cooked directly with the curry, no overnight soaking, no separate boil. The pressure cooker softens the pulses to creamy tenderness in 35 minutes and the spiced tomato base infuses them as they cook.
sourceNotes: Chickpea curry (chana masala) is a Punjabi home staple. The traditional method soaks the chickpeas overnight and simmers them for two hours; the pressure-cooker shortcut is the standard in modern Indian home kitchens with multi-cookers. Cooking the chickpeas directly in the masala is unusual in Indian cooking (most recipes cook chickpeas separately and add them to the spiced sauce), but works in the pressure cooker because the long high-pressure phase melds the flavours without overcooking either component.

content.text: Dried chickpeas have a deeper, nuttier flavour than tinned and a creamier texture once cooked. The pressure cooker is what makes them practical on a weeknight: 35 minutes from dry to tender, with no overnight soak. The skins do not slip off (a frequent complaint about tinned) and they hold their shape in the sauce.

The order of operations matters. Spices bloom in oil first to release their aromatic compounds. Onions and tomatoes cook down to a thick masala. Only then do the chickpeas and water join, so the pressure cycle infuses the pulses as they soften. Doing it the other way around (chickpeas first, masala layered on top) gives you the right ingredients in the wrong relationship.
A 6-litre pressure cooker with a sauté function. Whole basmati rice or warm flatbreads to serve.
Bloom the spices, build the masala

Set the cooker to sauté. Warm {{vegetable-oil}} of oil. Add {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds and let them sizzle for 30 seconds until fragrant. Add the chopped onions and cook for 8 minutes, stirring often, until they take on a deep golden colour. Stir in {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger, {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, and {{chilli-green}} of chopped green chilli; cook for one minute. Add {{turmeric}} of turmeric, {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander, and {{garam-masala}} of garam masala; stir for 30 seconds.

Pour in {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tomatoes. Cook for 5 minutes, breaking the tomatoes down with a wooden spoon, until the mixture thickens and the oil starts to separate at the edges. Add {{chickpeas-dried}} of dried chickpeas, {{water}} of hot water, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Stir well.

Lock the lid and cook at high pressure for 35 minutes. Let the pressure release naturally for 15 minutes, then quick-release any remaining pressure. Open the lid; the chickpeas should crush easily between thumb and finger.

If the sauce is thinner than you would like, set the cooker back to sauté and reduce for 5 minutes. Taste and adjust the salt. Scatter the chopped {{coriander}} of coriander leaves over the top. Serve with rice or flatbread and {{lemon}} wedge of lemon for squeezing.

items.fix: Reseal and pressure-cook for another 15 minutes, then natural-release. Buy chickpeas with the most recent best-before date for the most reliable results.
items.cause: Old chickpeas (past their best by several months) take longer to soften, even under pressure.
items.symptom: Chickpeas are still firm in the middle

items.fix: Reduce uncovered on sauté mode for 5 to 10 minutes. Mash a quarter of the chickpeas against the side of the pot to thicken naturally.
items.cause: Used too much water for the chickpea-to-tomato ratio.

items.fix: Add the water in stages, scraping the base each time. The cooker needs liquid throughout the cook to generate steam without scorching.
items.cause: Onion or tomato stuck to the base after the masala stage.

items.fix: Add another half teaspoon of garam masala at the end and stir through. Garam masala loses its aromatics after long cooking; the second addition restores them.
items.cause: Spices not bloomed long enough or under-seasoned.

content.text: Stir 200 g of fresh spinach in at the end and cook for 2 minutes until wilted for a saag chana. Add a splash of coconut milk for a Keralan-style curry. Use black chickpeas (kala chana) for a denser, earthier dish, extend the pressure time to 45 minutes.

Chana masala is a Punjabi everyday curry, the kind cooked on Sunday for the working week ahead. The pressure cooker is the standard kitchen appliance across the Indian subcontinent, and the recipe is built around it; the Anglo-Indian habit of using tinned chickpeas dilutes both the flavour and the texture. Dried pulses cost a third of tinned and deliver more dish for the same money.
